News Audience Analytics Dashboard: Content Performance & Reader Engagement

An editorial intelligence report built on the MIND News Dataset

Lindsey Hornberger

2026-01-12

News Audience Analytics

Overview

Audience engagement summary — MIND News Dataset
Metric Value
Total Sessions 156,964
Unique Users 50,000
Overall CTR 4.04%
Avg Articles Shown per Session 37.2
Avg Clicks per Session 1.51

About this report

This report analyzes reader engagement patterns from the Microsoft News Dataset (MIND-small), a benchmark dataset drawn from anonymized behavior logs of Microsoft News users collected between October 12 and November 22, 2019. The dataset contains a random sample of 50,000 users, their click histories, and impression logs across 51,002 unique articles.

Note on weekday coverage: Friday does not appear in this dataset’s collection window. All weekday analyses reflect Monday–Thursday and Saturday–Sunday patterns only.

The analysis is structured to answer the questions a newsroom audience analyst asks daily:

  • Who is engaging — how readers segment by engagement depth
  • When they engage — hourly and weekday patterns that inform publish timing
  • What they click — which content categories and headline structures drive the highest CTR
  • What it means — plain-language editorial recommendations drawn from the data

Audience Behavior

Timing & Patterns

Content Performance

Editorial Insights

Key findings for editorial strategy

`{r insights-setup, echo=FALSE}

1. Publish high-performing content during peak engagement windows

Reader intent peaks at 8:00 — CTR during this hour is 4.29%, compared to a daily average of 4.02%. Tuesday is the strongest day of the week for engagement.

Recommendation: Schedule high-priority content — particularly from top-performing categories — to publish during morning peak windows. Breaking news aside, evergreen and feature content should be timed to align with demonstrated audience attention patterns.

2. Music drives the strongest engagement — lean into it

Music has the highest CTR in the dataset at 5.94%, meaningfully outperforming Travel at the bottom (2.63%).

Recommendation: Audience interest is not evenly distributed across coverage areas. Editors should factor category-level engagement benchmarks into coverage planning — not to chase clicks, but to ensure high-investment content is reaching receptive audiences and to identify undercovered topics with demonstrated reader appetite.

3. Question headlines underperform declarative headlines by 37.4%

Headlines framed as questions achieve a CTR of 2.56% versus 4.09% for declarative headlines — a 37.4% drop.

Recommendation: Declarative headlines outperform question framing in this feed environment, suggesting readers respond more strongly to authoritative statements than open-ended prompts. Editors should favor clear, direct headline construction over question framing.

4. A small core of loyal readers drives outsized engagement

The top 20% of users by session frequency account for 55.6% of all clicks. The majority of users appear only once in the dataset, representing a significant re-engagement opportunity.

Recommendation: Retention strategy should prioritize converting one-time visitors into returning readers. Newsletter sign-up prompts, personalized content recommendations, and push notification opt-ins are highest-value interventions for this segment — turning occasional browsers into the loyal core that drives the majority of engagement.

About this analysis

This report was built using the Microsoft News Dataset (MIND), a large-scale benchmark dataset of anonymized impression logs from Microsoft News. The dataset contains 156,964 sessions, 50,000 unique users, and 51,002 articles across multiple content categories.

Analytical methods include session-level engagement segmentation, CTR analysis by content category and headline structure, temporal engagement modeling, and user loyalty classification. All visualizations built in R using ggplot2.

Data source: Microsoft News Dataset (MIND) — https://msnews.github.io/
